compiled_state.title: Allow coding sub-agents to use worktree isolation
compiled_state.description:
## Context

DSO plugin users running orchestration skills (sprint, debug-everything, fix-bug) dispatch multiple sub-agents per epic, but all sub-agents share the orchestrators working directory. This causes: (1) parallel agents risk file conflicts, (2) changes batch into large commits harder to review reliably, (3) review/test gates run on combined changes rather than individual units of work.

This is Epic A of a two-epic sequence. Epic A delivers the full vertical slice for worktree isolation with serial review. Epic B (separate ticket) scales to parallel review with Clash conflict prevention.

## Success Criteria

1. Orchestration skills dispatch implementation sub-agents with isolation: worktree, giving each agent its own working directory — verified by test asserting sub-agent git rev-parse --show-toplevel differs from orchestrators root
2. Worktree-isolation-guard updated from categorical deny to auth-file allowlist: orchestrator writes /tmp/worktree-isolation-authorized-<session-id> before dispatch, guard checks marker — verified by test showing authorized agents pass and unauthorized agents blocked
3. When implementation sub-agents return, orchestrator reviews and tests each worktree serially, running review/test sub-agents in that worktrees cwd (each worktree naturally gets its own ARTIFACTS_DIR via REPO_ROOT hash) — verified by review producing reviewer-findings.json in worktrees artifact dir
4. Each worktree that passes review produces its own commit in its worktree branch and merges into the session branch immediately (first-pass-first-merge by completion order) — verified by git log showing one commit per worktree with no combined batches
5. Merge conflicts detected at merge time are flagged — conflicting worktree queued for re-implementation or user escalation — verified by injected conflict test asserting ticket comment CONFLICT: worktree <id> blocked
6. A worktree.isolation_enabled config key in dso-config.conf enables/disables worktree isolation (default: true); when disabled, fallback to shared-directory mode — verified by config toggle
7. Worktree retention protocol: orchestrator retains worktree until merge complete; cleanup only after successful merge or explicit release

## Approach

Pattern B (orchestrator-controlled): Sub-agents dispatch with Claude Code native isolation: worktree and implement code changes only — no review, test, or commit in sub-agent. When agents return, orchestrator reviews and tests each worktree serially (one at a time), commits in the worktree branch, then merges into session branch. Auth-file allowlist pattern for the isolation guard: orchestrator writes temp marker, guard checks it.

## Spike Findings (verified in session)

- ARTIFACTS_DIR: naturally isolated per worktree because get_artifacts_dir() hashes REPO_ROOT, which differs per worktree. No code changes needed for artifact isolation.
- Diff hash: stable because review + commit both happen in the worktree (same HEAD). Merge commit into session branch triggers merge-state detection, and review gate skips merge commits (existing behavior). The hash mismatch concern is a non-issue.
- reviewer-findings.json: no race condition. Parallel worktrees write to different ARTIFACTS_DIRs automatically.
- Guard allowlist: auth-file approach is feasible. Guard receives full tool_input JSON. Orchestrator writes /tmp/worktree-isolation-authorized-<session-id> before dispatch; guard checks for marker file. Unforgeable by prompt injection.
- Dynamic dispatch: Claude Code isolation: worktree parameter is documented for Agent tool. The claude-code#26923 exit-code reliability concern is specific to exit code 2, not to the JSON permissionDecision mechanism the guard uses.

## Scenario Analysis

17 scenarios survived blue team filter (from 27 red team). Key resolutions:
- Clash non-propagation: deferred to Epic B (Clash not in Epic A scope)
- Sequential merge deadlock: serial review eliminates this in Epic A; Epic B adds timeout
- Branch base drift: review + commit in worktree before merge; standard git 3-way merge handles drift
- reviewer-findings.json race: naturally isolated per worktree via ARTIFACTS_DIR
- CI fallback: worktree.isolation_enabled config key
- Guard blocker: auth-file allowlist pattern
- Review gate hash mismatch: non-issue per spike findings
- Merge-state misidentification: merge commit correctly detected by existing merge-state.sh
- Test gate centrality: orchestrator writes test-gate-status in worktree before commit
- Version bump: merge-to-main runs once at session end, not per worktree
- Worktree cleanup before review: explicit retention protocol
- Ticket system access: sub-agents dont touch tickets (Pattern B); orchestrator owns all ticket ops

## Dependencies

- Depends on dso-t7af (Remove 5-sub-agent cap): can ship with existing cap retained; throttling epic unlocks full potential
- Coordinate with 4c54-4205 (Interface contracts for parallel agent coordination)

## Research Findings

### Claude Code Native Worktree Isolation
- Trigger: Novel architectural pattern
- Query: Claude Code isolation:worktree sub-agent behavior
- Sources: code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows, code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents
- Key insight: First-class support since v2.1.49. Framework handles creation, cwd injection, cleanup. Plugin files available in worktree (fresh checkout). Plugin hooks do NOT propagate to plugin sub-agents.

### Multi-Agent Worktree Patterns
- Trigger: Novel architectural pattern
- Query: How agentic tools merge isolated worktree changes
- Sources: github.com/Duatadmin/bender-pipeline, github.com/justinjdev/fellowship, github.com/clash-sh/clash
- Key insight: Pattern B (sub-agent implements, orchestrator reviews/merges) is most common. Sequential merge-back is universal. Fellowship achieves 95% phase-gate compliance with hook enforcement vs 33% prompt-only.

### Planning Intelligence Log

- Web research (Step 2.6): triggered
  - Bright-line conditions: external integration (Clash), unfamiliar dependency, novel architectural pattern
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): triggered
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 17
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: none

comments.body: Complete multiple non-conflicting changes in parallel, but review and commit them separately in parallel rather than in a batch. Roll this into worktree isolation support for sub-agents.
comments.author: Claude

comments.body: Vision note from brainstorm (dso-t7af): Eventually, each sub-agent dispatched by sprint/debug-everything should work in a separate worktree using worktree isolation, running the code review process and testing in parallel on each sub-agent worktree before merging results back into the session's worktree. This would eliminate file-conflict risk from parallel agents and allow true independent validation per sub-agent. This is out of scope for the parallel agents epic (dso-t7af) — in the short run we accept the risk of larger commits with increased parallelism.
comments.author: Claude

comments.body: Adversarial review complete: Red team: 16 findings. Blue team: 5 rejected, 11 accepted. Applied: 0 new stories, 5 modified done definitions, 4 new considerations, 1 dependency, 1 doc update.
comments.author: Claude

comments.body: Epic complete: all 6 stories closed, all 23 tasks completed, validation PASS, branch merged to main (v1.4.7)
comments.author: Test
